description: Regression test cases for the launch-package skill. Two blueprints: one B2B SaaS, one consumer app. Each test case specifies the input blueprint, the expected Narrative Spine fields, and assertions against key output properties.

test_cases.name: B2B SaaS — Launch Copy Generator
test_cases.description: A B2B SaaS tool for early-stage founders that generates complete, narrative-consistent launch packages from a product brief.

blueprint.icp: Growth-stage SaaS founders with 1–5 person marketing teams launching their first or second product
blueprint.problem: Writing launch copy takes 3–5 days and still comes out inconsistent — the landing page sounds like one product, the email sequence sounds like another, and the pitch deck is a third voice. By launch day, the team can't agree on what problem they're solving.
blueprint.solution: Acme generates a complete launch package — 6 deliverables — from a 10-field product brief. All 6 assets share a Narrative Spine, so the landing page hero headline, email E1 subject, and pitch deck punchline are different expressions of the same sentence.
blueprint.unique_mechanism: Narrative Spine derivation — a shared context object derived before any template is touched, ensuring all 6 deliverables draw from the same canonical language
blueprint.traction: 320 beta users, 38% 7-day retention, growing 20% MoM over 8 weeks
testimonials: Cut our launch prep from 4 days to 4 hours — Alex Chen, Head of Marketing, Linktree
First time our landing page and email sequence sounded like the same company — Jamie Park, Founder, Buildstack
blueprint.why_now: LLMs can now generate coherent copy at scale, but without a narrative layer, they produce content without story. The tooling exists; the framework to make it consistent didn't — until now.

known_challenges: Competition axes must be 'narrative consistency vs. speed' — not generic 'price vs. features'
E1 and E2 must not mention 'Acme' anywhere — verify explicitly
Proof sections must use real beta numbers — no invented traction
Canonical CTA must propagate consistently to all 6 deliverables

test_cases.name: Consumer App — Personal Finance Habit Tracker
test_cases.description: A consumer mobile app that helps people in their 20s–30s build saving habits through micro-commitments and social accountability.

blueprint.icp: People in their 20s and 30s who earn enough to save but spend it anyway — often because saving feels abstract and the reward is too far away
blueprint.problem: Most people know they should be saving more but don't. Not because they lack money — because saving money today for a reward in 20 years feels meaningless. Every app shows them their balance. None of them makes saving feel real right now.
blueprint.solution: Stash turns saving into a social habit. You make a micro-commitment — $5 to a shared goal — and your friend matches it or challenges it. The accountability is immediate; the progress is visible; the reward isn't abstract anymore.
blueprint.unique_mechanism: Peer micro-commitments — saving triggers only when a friend accepts your challenge, making the act of saving a social event rather than a solitary decision
blueprint.pricing: free, $4.99/mo Premium for goal templates and analytics
blueprint.traction: 1,200 waitlist signups, 4.8/5 average rating from 80 TestFlight users
testimonials: Saved $800 in 3 months because I didn't want to let my friend down — Maya R., 26, Chicago
Finally saved for that trip — we both did it together — Tom K., 29, Austin
team_bios: Sara Lin, CEO — previously product lead at Robinhood (0 to 1M users)
Alex Mori, CTO — built payments infrastructure at Stripe for 4 years
blueprint.why_now: Gen Z and millennials are the first generation to grow up with social apps as primary behavior-change tools. Fitness habits moved to social (Strava). Sleep moved to social (Rise). Saving hasn't — yet.

known_challenges: ICP is consumer — tone must shift from B2B confidence to personal warmth without losing directness
Unique mechanism (peer micro-commitments) is behavioral, not technical — demo script must show the social interaction, not just the UI
Competition axes: not 'budgeting apps vs. investment apps' — the real axis is 'solo vs. social savings behavior'
Consumer testimonials should use first names only (no last name + company) — this is acceptable for consumer products
Product Hunt tagline must not mention 'social saving app' as a category label — lead with the outcome

evaluation_instructions.how_to_run: For each test case: (1) provide the blueprint to the skill, (2) confirm the Narrative Spine matches expected_spine fields, (3) verify all assertions in the assertions block, (4) run validate-lengths.py on the generated templates directory, (5) record quality_gate verdict.
evaluation_instructions.pass_criteria: A test case passes if: (a) all structural assertions match, (b) validate-lengths.py exits 0, (c) quality_gate verdict matches expected_verdict.
evaluation_instructions.fail_criteria: A test case fails if: (a) any structural assertion fails, (b) validate-lengths.py exits 1, (c) spine fields don't satisfy expected_spine patterns, (d) verdict is worse than expected.
regression_notes: tc-001 is the canonical B2B case — use it first. If it fails, do not proceed to tc-002.
tc-002 tests tone-shift from B2B to consumer. Voice consistency (N1) is the hardest dimension here.
Both test cases should produce LAUNCH-READY verdicts with all traction fields populated.
If traction fields are emptied from the blueprint, expected verdict downgrades to REVISE-THEN-LAUNCH.
